6:41 p.m.
When trying the new storage, you need to export your config and settings first then re-import as it uses a different storage. the original storage should remain untouched if you want to revert, but no promise. As always, if you have weird behavior, try a factory reset (in the app and after exporting everything) then re-import.

12:40 p.m.

12:41 p.m.

recrof
maybe bluetooth disconnect is blocking?
Mystic 2025-09-02 12:47 p.m.
in my case on wio tracker 1110 its not due to anything blocking or bluetooth related (from what i can tell), it's that randomly all subsequent messages will be shifted 4 bytes to the right, meaning they start with null bytes. those shifts add together over time - if i leave it running after the issue first starts then eventually they end up getting shifted 8 bytes, and presumably 12, 16, etc. to me it looks like this shift originates from radiolib, as in nothing in the meshcore firmware is causing that shift, its written by radiolib's readData. the lr1110 does shift the buffer base on each received message, that's mentioned in a comment in radiolib, but as far as i can tell that's handled properly - it first asks the radio for the offset in the rx buffer and the length of the message, then reads from that offset. makes me wonder a little if its an issue with the radio itself, but surely someone would've noticed that. i've just updated my radio from version 0x0307 -> 0x0401, now waiting to see if the issue reoccurs. if it does, i might test this board as a repeater, maybe something companion-specific is triggering this issue somewhere. could be a good idea for others on t1000e to check their radio firmware version and update if possible?
Mystic
in my case on wio tracker 1110 its not due to anything blocking or bluetooth related (from what i can tell), it's that randomly all subsequent messages will be shifted 4 bytes to the right, meaning they start with null bytes. those shifts add together over time - if i leave it running after the issue first starts then eventually they end up getting shifted 8 bytes, and presumably 12, 16, etc. to me it looks like this shift originates from radiolib, as in nothing in the meshcore firmware is causing that shift, its written by radiolib's readData. the lr1110 does shift the buffer base on each received message, that's mentioned in a comment in radiolib, but as far as i can tell that's handled properly - it first asks the radio for the offset in the rx buffer and the length of the message, then reads from that offset. makes me wonder a little if its an issue with the radio itself, but surely someone would've noticed that. i've just updated my radio from version 0x0307 -> 0x0401, now waiting to see if the issue reoccurs. if it does, i might test this board as a repeater, maybe something companion-specific is triggering this issue somewhere. could be a good idea for others on t1000e to check their radio firmware version and update if possible?
Mystic 2025-09-02 4:59 p.m.
firmware update has not helped, the issue has returned. i commented out the clearRxBuffer call in readData in radiolib and now i'm getting fragments of previous packets or something instead of the null bytes which confirms the bytes are being read from the rx buffer of the lr1110, not being added somewhere upstream, so seems to be something related to the buffer base, no idea why its happening or how to fix it though
1
recrof 2025-09-05 2:08 a.m.
t1000e repeater still alive
recrof
t1000e repeater still alive
Mystic 2025-09-05 12:39 p.m.
i just had a thought, no idea why this didn't occur to me before. with the issue i'm seeing on my wio tracker 1110 dev board, it's highly likely that repeaters would be fine bc they usually tx after they rx and txing resets the buffer base and the issue clears, so it could well be the same issue. i believe it's not the actual txing that's resetting the buffer base but some change of state that can theoretically be performed after every rx, but that feels really hacky and shouldn't be needed. has anyone with this t1000e deafness on companion been able to view debug logs over serial while the issue is present to confirm if its the same thing i'm seeing?
recrof 2025-09-05 12:40 p.m.
I guess there are few people that can test that.. my t1000 is pretty high up, so I can't "de-repeaterize" it soon
Mystic
i just had a thought, no idea why this didn't occur to me before. with the issue i'm seeing on my wio tracker 1110 dev board, it's highly likely that repeaters would be fine bc they usually tx after they rx and txing resets the buffer base and the issue clears, so it could well be the same issue. i believe it's not the actual txing that's resetting the buffer base but some change of state that can theoretically be performed after every rx, but that feels really hacky and shouldn't be needed. has anyone with this t1000e deafness on companion been able to view debug logs over serial while the issue is present to confirm if its the same thing i'm seeing?

support other battery chemisrties · Issue #398 · meshcore-dev/Mes...

Mystic 2025-09-14 1:44 p.m.
i wouldn't have noticed if i weren't looking at the serial console. when it gets into this state, it thinks all msgs are type=0 route=F so retransmits, and the tx gets it out of the bugged state

i wouldn't have noticed if i weren't looking at the serial console. when it gets into this state, it thinks all msgs are type=0 route=F so retransmits, and the tx gets it out of the bugged state

ded (SalishMesh)
Testing some IRQ changes Che suggested
recrof 2025-09-20 4:56 p.m.

ded (SalishMesh) 2025-09-20 5:22 p.m.
We have apparently been using the wrong IRQ flags for lr1110
5:22 p.m.
https://github.com/oltaco/MeshCore/commit/274b6dc2cb50e011b2445af5c1a5dbb6b3d3d87c
LR1110: fix IRQ flags · oltaco/MeshCore@274b6dc
ded (SalishMesh)
We have apparently been using the wrong IRQ flags for lr1110
recrof 2025-09-20 5:52 p.m.
ded (SalishMesh) 2025-09-20 5:54 p.m.
Lol if this works, very much so
ded (SalishMesh)
Lol if this works, very much so
recrof 2025-09-20 5:57 p.m.
does @Mystic know?
ded (SalishMesh) 2025-09-20 6:00 p.m.
I'm not sure, Che just thought it might be a long shot and I offered to test the theory
Mystic 2025-09-20 6:49 p.m.
hmmm
6:49 p.m.
interesting
Mystic 2025-09-20 7:00 p.m.
so we were effectively checking IRQ_TX_DONE when we shouldn't've been and not IRQ_SYNC_WORD_HEADER_VALID when we should've been for isReceiving
7:01 p.m.
i don't think that would've caused the issues i saw but i would love to be proven wrong
7:01 p.m.
i shall apply that fix on my repeater tomorrow

Mystic 2025-09-21 6:30 a.m.

1
6:30 a.m.
if you do want to see the raw packets you can add a little bit of code to logRxRaw
6:30 a.m.
Serial.printf("logRxRaw: snr=%.2f rssi=%.2f len=%d data=", snr, rssi, len); mesh::Utils::printHex(Serial, raw, len); Serial.print("\n"); (edited)

recrof 2025-09-22 1:54 a.m.
interesting
recrof 2025-09-22 2:33 a.m.
tagging @Scott and @FDLamotte - our lr1110 radio implementation is shifting packet bytes to the right after some time and it's reset after tx. have idea where this might happen?
2:35 a.m.
the airtime computation stuff might also be relevant to this?
Scott 2025-09-22 2:50 a.m.
Sounds like FIFO queue pointer thing(?) Dunno
recrof
tagging @Scott and @FDLamotte - our lr1110 radio implementation is shifting packet bytes to the right after some time and it's reset after tx. have idea where this might happen?
FDLamotte 2025-09-22 3:32 a.m.
I agree on the symptoms, but have no clue
recrof 2025-09-22 3:35 a.m.
confirmed on my t1000 as well
3:40 a.m.
so I guess this might be a radiolib issue then? (edited)
recrof
so I guess this might be a radiolib issue then? (edited)
Scott 2025-09-22 3:51 a.m.
Most likely
recrof 2025-09-22 3:56 a.m.
we need to create test case that will only use radiolib and as basic init as possible, so we can confirm this is indeed issue there. and I guess MT doesn't have this problem because every node tx-es regularly - the same reason they avoided issues with AGC lockup (edited)
Mystic 2025-09-22 5:00 a.m.
the radio does indeed shift the position of the packet in the rx buffer for each msg, but from what i can tell radiolib is correctly reading the current offset from the radio then reading from that offset. here:
5:02 a.m.
in my testing the buffer base moves by the length of the previous msg, not by 4 bytes as the comment says, but clearly 4 bytes is significant here
recrof
confirmed on my t1000 as well
ViezeVingertjes 2025-09-22 1:01 p.m.
Are you able to reproduce it consistently? (edited)
ded (SalishMesh) 2025-09-22 1:09 p.m.
We don't know what the cause is yet, just that it happens consistently there might be a pattern examining the raw packets preceding the event
ViezeVingertjes
Are you able to reproduce it consistently? (edited)
recrof 2025-09-22 1:15 p.m.
yeah, you just need to wait long enough and it will surface (edited)
1:16 p.m.
@Mystic did you try to startReceive() like we do with agc lockup?
ViezeVingertjes 2025-09-22 1:52 p.m.
Anyone willing to give this a try? It's based on some old radiolib issue that hinted to RxDone/TxDone IRQ ambiguity... long shot, but so far it looks like it could explain the buffer issue for the LR1110. https://drive.google.com/drive/folders/1eClOL8yJYyQL3_OZb7058TTKYo20Nt56
1:54 p.m.
I did not notice the drift per se; but with this modification, i do notice that after idling, the ping to repeater (from this companion) instantly returns (well, 800ms or so, like normal here), where normally the first ping would take 5-7 seconds. (edited)
ded (SalishMesh) 2025-09-22 1:55 p.m.
Interesting
1:55 p.m.
I can try it later today
ded (SalishMesh)
I can try it later today
ViezeVingertjes 2025-09-22 1:59 p.m.
Would be great; the theory is that in RadioLibWrapper::recvRaw it would do a readData while not in STATE_RX state thus messing up the buffer.
1:59 p.m.
But maybe im person 999 trying that solution haha... maybe we should keep track of an attempt log or something.
ded (SalishMesh) 2025-09-22 2:01 p.m.
Does it have packet logging on?
ded (SalishMesh)
Does it have packet logging on?
ViezeVingertjes 2025-09-22 2:03 p.m.

recrof 2025-09-22 2:57 p.m.
@Mystic https://github.com/meshcore-dev/MeshCore/blob/47c57a52cc3081f8b3e7f44c6770a322925221bd/src/helpers/radiolib/RadioLibWrappers.cpp#L56
MeshCore/src/helpers/radiolib/RadioLibWrappers.cpp at 47c57a52cc308...
A new lightweight, hybrid routing mesh protocol for packet radios - meshcore-dev/MeshCore
ViezeVingertjes 2025-09-22 3:35 p.m.
Tossing it in here, just in case @Mystic or @FDLamotte have the chance to test. https://github.com/meshcore-dev/MeshCore/pull/816 cant guarantee yet that it will fix the buffer issue, but all seems to fit / explainable if it would be caused by the same.
ViezeVingertjes
Tossing it in here, just in case @Mystic or @FDLamotte have the chance to test. https://github.com/meshcore-dev/MeshCore/pull/816 cant guarantee yet that it will fix the buffer issue, but all seems to fit / explainable if it would be caused by the same.
FDLamotte 2025-09-22 3:38 p.m.
interesting ... this would make sense ...
ded (SalishMesh) 2025-09-22 3:39 p.m.

Mystic 2025-09-22 3:40 p.m.

ded (SalishMesh) 2025-09-22 3:40 p.m.
You would think it would create other deaf devices though too right? As it's not lr1110 specific
3:41 p.m.
Or maybe this radio is just more susceptible
ViezeVingertjes 2025-09-22 3:53 p.m.
The difference is in their readData implementation: https://github.com/jgromes/RadioLib/blob/master/src/modules/LR11x0/LR11x0.cpp https://github.com/jgromes/RadioLib/blob/master/src/modules/SX126x/SX126x.cpp It's probably messed up by getPacketLength in specific for LR1110
3:55 p.m.
Would make sense, as they had this issue at some point and fixed it, but not for the LR1110 i would say
3:56 p.m.
Or well, fixed it would mean it was broken, but handled it more specifically to prevent it from happening.
ded (SalishMesh) 2025-09-22 4:02 p.m.
Awesome, well I'm excited to test it

ohoh (edited)

like you left the radio in other location and when you returned.. public channel queue was empty

ded (SalishMesh) 2025-09-24 10:17 a.m.
This was my first thought, not that I really know what's going on, but I wondered if it was a corrupt or improperly decoded packet that somehow breaks further packets. ded (SalishMesh)
This was my first thought, not that I really know what's going on, but I wondered if it was a corrupt or improperly decoded packet that somehow breaks further packets. ViezeVingertjes 2025-09-27 7:26 a.m.
7:28 a.m.
Maybe another approach; cant we work our way around it, by not truncating the packet and then trimming those null bytes? Or is that data that fell off already gone at this point?
7:28 a.m.
As i assume, this RAW is already after the truncating(?)
7:29 a.m.
Its not pretty, but perhaps we can then save the packet and reset when those null bytes are present or something?
7:30 a.m.

ViezeVingertjes
Maybe another approach; cant we work our way around it, by not truncating the packet and then trimming those null bytes? Or is that data that fell off already gone at this point?
Mystic 2025-09-27 8:09 a.m.

  • he gets the T1000 to DFU, USB-SERIAL CH340 (COM3) appears in device manager
  • python + adafruit-nrfutil installed PS C:\Users\orsib\AppData\Roaming\Python\Python313\Scripts> .\adafruit-nrfutil.exe --verbose dfu serial --package C:\Users\orsib\Downloads\t1000_e_bootloader-0.9.1-5-g488711a_s140_7.3.0.zip -p COM4 -b 115200 --singlebank --touch 1200
Upgrading target on COM4 with DFU package C:\Users\orsib\Downloads\t1000_e_bootloader-0.9.1-5-g488711a_s140_7.3.0.zip. Flow control is disabled, Single bank, Touch 1200 Touched serial port COM3 Opened serial port COM3 Starting DFU upgrade of type 3, SoftDevice size: 152728, bootloader size: 39000, application size: 0 Sending DFU start packet Timed out waiting for acknowledgement from device.
3:31 a.m.

8472 2025-10-04 12:09 p.m.
Sorry in advance, probably wasting everyone's time, but I ran the codebase through GPT-5 Codex in VS Code and asked it to analyse the issue with 0x00 being preappended.. If it helps(?) This was the analysis: Why the extra 0x00 shows up When the dispatcher calls _radio->readData(), RadioLib pulls bytes starting from the LR1110’s current FIFO read pointer. If that pointer isn’t at zero, whatever sits there (often the status byte, which is 0x00) becomes the first element we see—hence the prepended zero. In our LR1110 wrapper we never reprogram the buffer base addresses after begin(), and isReceivingPacket() reads/clears IRQ flags before the dispatcher drains the FIFO. Together they let the FIFO pointer stay advanced from the previous frame. So the zero isn’t “added” by MeshCore; it’s the modem returning the byte that lives at the FIFO offset where we started reading. Why a transmit clears it RadioLibWrapper::onSendFinished() calls _radio->finishTransmit(). On LR1110 that routine re-arms the radio for receive, which internally resets the RX base/back into a sane position (and often re-issues SetBufferBaseAddress). With the FIFO pointers realigned, the next readData() begins at offset 0, so the phantom 0x00 vanishes—until the same misaligned receive flow happens again. In short: the “added” zero is the LR1110 handing us the wrong slot because we clear IRQs and never reset the FIFO between receives; finishing a TX implicitly reinitializes the radio, snapping the pointer back to zero. The fix is to explicitly reset RX base addresses (or avoid clearing the IRQ before we drain the FIFO) so every receive starts from buffer index 0 without needing a transmit to recover. Lining up the pieces: this only surfaces after the repeater has been up for a while because the LR1110 keeps walking its RX FIFO pointer forward with every packet, and we never explicitly reset it during a steady-state receive-only session.
12:09 p.m.
What’s happening over hours Each successful startReceive → readData cycle advances the chip’s internal FIFO cursor by the payload length it just handed us. Our code reissues SetBufferBaseAddress (indirectly) only at boot or right after a transmit. In pure repeater mode we can stay in RX for hours without ever transmitting, so that pointer just keeps climbing. The LR1110’s RX buffer is a ring; once the cursor wraps, RadioLib’s readData implementation begins at the status byte slot (0x00) instead of the true start of the next frame. That’s when you see the prepended zero, and the dispatcher immediately rejects the packet. Any outbound send (even a tiny one) calls finishTransmit(), which reinitializes the FIFO base and resets the cursor—so the issue “goes away” until the pointer drifts again. Why it takes time The pointer has to accumulate enough offset to lap the ring buffer. With 200–300 byte packets and a 256-byte FIFO window, that can take hundreds of packets—i.e., hours on a quiet mesh—before the wrap-around lands exactly where the status byte gets exposed. If traffic is lighter (or periodic beacons do force a transmit), you can go much longer without hitting the problem; once you’re strictly listening, the drift eventually catches up. Fix direction Explicitly reset the RX/TX base addresses whenever we return to receive (or at least every few packets), or avoid clearing IRQs before readData() consumes the frame. Either change keeps the FIFO pointer from drifting for hours and eliminates the mysterious leading zero.

hehe meh, im going into the same rabbithole again, i ended up in readData & getPacketLength once more.
1:19 p.m.

1:20 p.m.
"the offset is needed since LR11x0 seems to move the buffer base by 4 bytes on every packet" pretty sure thats the 4 bytes we see
Mystic 2025-10-06 1:21 p.m.
i believe it can theoretically be worked around by detecting when the issue occurs and reading 4 (or more) bytes ahead in the rx buffer
1:21 p.m.
but it shouldn't be doing that in the first place
ViezeVingertjes
"the offset is needed since LR11x0 seems to move the buffer base by 4 bytes on every packet" pretty sure thats the 4 bytes we see
Mystic 2025-10-06 1:22 p.m.
yeah i saw that comment but its a bit weird, from what i saw it moves the buffer base by the packet's length on every packet
1:22 p.m.
so first packet of length 30 would be at offset 0, then next packet would be at offset 30
1:22 p.m.
but interesting that 4 bytes is mentioned
ViezeVingertjes 2025-10-06 1:25 p.m.
Yes, and also that it moves the buffer by 4 every packet, so basically it can accumilate

2:03 p.m.
2:04 p.m.

2:09 p.m.

2:10 p.m.

those crc errors might be significant imho

2:56 p.m.

3:01 p.m.
@jgromes I know it's a long shot, but I never seen this on my lr1121 radio(t3s3), but it happens on t1000e and wio tracker 1110. my hunch is it's only on lr1110
jgromes 2025-10-06 3:01 p.m.
OK, thanks. My guess is that it's caused by the combination of the 0-length packet and the CRC errors. If I force skipping the offset update I do see the 4 bytes start adding up
jgromes
OK, thanks. My guess is that it's caused by the combination of the 0-length packet and the CRC errors. If I force skipping the offset update I do see the 4 bytes start adding up
recrof 2025-10-06 3:02 p.m.
is there chance it's caused by some kind of race condition?
3:03 p.m.
not 100% sure if this is completely avoided by interrupt handling
jgromes 2025-10-06 3:03 p.m.
Unlikely, as I'm only running it in a single thread
ViezeVingertjes 2025-10-06 3:08 p.m.
https://github.com/jgromes/RadioLib/pull/1621/files I was currently looking into this direction, but i couldn't find any information about clearRxBuffer that confirms that it would actually reset the state in such way, that it would start fresh on the buffer.
jgromes 2025-10-06 3:09 p.m.
It probably doesn't according to the docs it just fills teh buffer with zeroes
ViezeVingertjes 2025-10-06 3:09 p.m.
Ah, check, then that attempt is also down the drain hehe...
jgromes 2025-10-06 3:10 p.m.
Apparently the way to set the buffer base pointers is with SetBufferBaseAddresses() which is mentioned precisely once in LR1110 and 1120 datasheets and is not actually documented
3:10 p.m.
Neither in docs, nor on Semtech's Github as far as I can tell
ViezeVingertjes 2025-10-06 3:10 p.m.
Of course it isnt, hehe. (edited)
3:14 p.m.
TX still did 'fix' it right? @recrof Isnt that basically a standBy -> set rx that reset's it then?
ViezeVingertjes
TX still did 'fix' it right? @recrof Isnt that basically a standBy -> set rx that reset's it then?
recrof 2025-10-06 3:14 p.m.
yeah, tx resets it
ViezeVingertjes

Mystic 2025-10-06 3:16 p.m.
skipping that does not cause any adverse effects
3:16 p.m.
already tried
3:16 p.m.
it just means instead of getting zeroes you get fragments of previous packets in the bytes before the shifted packet
ViezeVingertjes 2025-10-06 3:16 p.m.
Yes, it seems to only blank the buffer, not reset the pointers.
recrof
yeah, tx resets it
ViezeVingertjes 2025-10-06 3:17 p.m.

ViezeVingertjes

3:19 p.m.
like, not in the mc data, it handled by the radio i thought
3:19 p.m.

3:19 p.m.

ViezeVingertjes 2025-10-06 3:21 p.m.

3:22 p.m.

3:23 p.m.

3:24 p.m.
i think the crc errors are geniune crc errors of weak signals where the data wasn't quite read properly
ViezeVingertjes 2025-10-06 3:52 p.m.
Bit out of ideas except for hackyhacks here hehe.
Mystic
i don't think the crc errors are related
jgromes 2025-10-06 3:57 p.m.
Maybe not, but so far the only way I was able to trigger anthing resembling this issue was to skip the offset when reading from the Rx buffer. And then it just goes all over the place. A CRC error is the only thing I can think of that could change how this behaves. I'll try sending some packets with deliberately malformed CRCs to see if we can deliberately trigger it that way. If not then it's bad news for replicating it because I was not able to trigger it even with empty packet, in implicit header mode and no CRC, so pretty much just a preamble with a sync word.
3:58 p.m.
Having access to SetBufferBaseAddresses would help a lot, I'll see if we can figure out if it the command exists
4:00 p.m.
I also tried to reset the Rx pointer by reading out the rest of the buffer after a packet is received, but it seems to be ignoring that. But it's definitely somehow related to those 4 bytes that the Rx pointer seems to be moving by
4:00 p.m.
Semtech examples always read out the offset and apply it, so it is possible they never noticed this behavior
Mystic 2025-10-06 4:00 p.m.
i'm leaning towards it being an issue with the radio but what do i know lol
4:02 p.m.
if it helps at all i could probably connect my wio tracker 1110 (connected to outdoor antenna) and xiao esp32c3 sx1262 (with the little flexible antenna it comes with) to a machine you could ssh/rdp into for testing as it happens quite frequently for me
Mystic
if it helps at all i could probably connect my wio tracker 1110 (connected to outdoor antenna) and xiao esp32c3 sx1262 (with the little flexible antenna it comes with) to a machine you could ssh/rdp into for testing as it happens quite frequently for me
recrof 2025-10-06 4:03 p.m.
you're near london, right?
Mystic 2025-10-06 4:03 p.m.
no, in the north, but england yes
recrof 2025-10-06 4:04 p.m.
this must be pretty bad for t1000e owners.. they will get deaf pretty instantly
ViezeVingertjes 2025-10-06 4:11 p.m.
What would be the effect of a workaround like if(length == 0) { state = standby(); RADIOLIB_ASSERT(state); state = setRx(this->rxTimeout); RADIOLIB_ASSERT(state); } Would this have negative impact on anything right off the bat? (edited)
jgromes 2025-10-06 4:33 p.m.
Hard to guess. If you're looking for a quick-fix then you can detect whether the first 4 bytes are null and do the standby/rx cycle then (if there are no valid packets in Meshcore that would start like that).
che aporeps 🌮 2025-10-06 4:35 p.m.
I don't know anything but I'll put it out there anyway.. what happens if the radio receives a preamble or a valid header but nothing follows? I don't believe we currently have a manual timeout on this situation.
jgromes 2025-10-06 4:40 p.m.
I think what happens is that for some reason, the Rx pointer gets moved twice by 8 bytes in total, but the offset reported by the radio is only incremented once by 4 bytes. Almost as if it received two packets at the same time but only managed to process one of them. Of course the interesting question is what is the root cause of this and whether we can do anything about it, it definitely looks like an issue in the radio itself. So you guys can apply some sort of hotfix to get the devices working, while I (try to) get in touch with Semtech.
che aporeps 🌮
I don't know anything but I'll put it out there anyway.. what happens if the radio receives a preamble or a valid header but nothing follows? I don't believe we currently have a manual timeout on this situation.
jgromes 2025-10-06 4:40 p.m.
I tried that and it seems to process it "correctly", in that it triggers an IRQ and reports a 0-length packet
1
recrof
but I see that @Mystic is also pretty quick in triggering it
ded (SalishMesh) 2025-10-06 4:41 p.m.
I'm cut off now from local noise, I can get out mostly but almost nothing coming in
ViezeVingertjes 2025-10-07 1:11 a.m.
So for now we will just implement that reset on the arrival of that len=0 packet(?) the null byte detection and then doing it, is probably too late right? as that would mean that packet is already missed.
ViezeVingertjes
So for now we will just implement that reset on the arrival of that len=0 packet(?) the null byte detection and then doing it, is probably too late right? as that would mean that packet is already missed.
jgromes 2025-10-07 1:33 a.m.
Not really, the packet is still in the buffer and can be rescued. If you read out a packet and detect it starts with 4 null bytes (again, assuming that this violates meshcore packets structure), you can just reset the radio, then in your program you shift the packet by 4 bytes and process it as normal. But I don't want to implement fixes like that in RadioLib until I have statement from Semtech on this issue.
1
ViezeVingertjes 2025-10-07 1:34 a.m.
Ah check! then that would also be a possibility. But wouldnt the reset on len=0 prevent it from happening? (better to prevent than to cure kinda thing?)
1:35 a.m.
Although that might give another attack vector or something, not sure hehe... just woke up, brain is not braining yet.
jgromes 2025-10-07 1:40 a.m.
We still don't know whether 0-length packet is really what triggers it. I was not able to do it just with packets without user data. So maybe it needs to be malformed in some way, or there needs to be some on-air collision.
ViezeVingertjes 2025-10-07 1:41 a.m.
True, but i think it is quite safe to say that it always appears as a len=0 packet (going by Mystic's logs that is)
1:41 a.m.
Its always that one, bam another 4 bytes
1:43 a.m.
It might not be a packet at all, theoretically(?) just something triggering in the wrong moment? But for that i dont know the specifics in detail enough to be fair. (edited)
1
jgromes
We still don't know whether 0-length packet is really what triggers it. I was not able to do it just with packets without user data. So maybe it needs to be malformed in some way, or there needs to be some on-air collision.
che aporeps 🌮 2025-10-07 1:49 a.m.
This was my thought, it's not really a 0 byte packet as such but something that triggers the radio's preamble detection or valid header detection, but then nothing follows at all.
ViezeVingertjes 2025-10-07 1:51 a.m.
Maybe a rx timeout and then a sprinkle of irq ambiguity. I have no clue, but im dying to know after all these time.
1
ViezeVingertjes
Maybe a rx timeout and then a sprinkle of irq ambiguity. I have no clue, but im dying to know after all these time.
che aporeps 🌮 2025-10-07 2:00 a.m.

Random Citizen 2025-10-07 3:35 a.m.

Mystic 2025-10-07 7:21 a.m.
yeah sounds about right
7:21 a.m.
bc first byte is 0x00
7:24 a.m.
but because it’s TRANSPORT_FLOOD it actually reads 4 bytes after the first byte for transport codes so then the path data lines up and probably the request data too if it is actually a request packet, assuming it’s only a 4 byte shift

Random Citizen 2025-10-07 3:07 p.m.
my t1000 is died of old age FS corruption again
jgromes 2025-10-07 3:09 p.m.
Finally had some success trying to induce this issue. I was able to get it shift by 4 bytes by transmitting with a second device on a nearby frequency. Even then it doesn't happen right away, there needs to be some on-air collision to trigger it. The collided packets manifest either as CRC errors, but sometimes as those 0-length packets
3:11 p.m.
Also reached out to Semtech if they have any comment/recommendation about this. Definitely looks like an issue in the radio and tbh I'm not sure how much will I be able to do from the RadioLib side
jgromes
Finally had some success trying to induce this issue. I was able to get it shift by 4 bytes by transmitting with a second device on a nearby frequency. Even then it doesn't happen right away, there needs to be some on-air collision to trigger it. The collided packets manifest either as CRC errors, but sometimes as those 0-length packets
recrof 2025-10-07 4:09 p.m.
that is pretty wild.. so this more-less makes this chipset unusable? or you think this can be patched by firmware update?
mcarper 2025-10-08 12:09 a.m.
Can we detect the bad rx in real time software side?
12:10 a.m.
Or is that only discovered when doing a tx
recrof
that is pretty wild.. so this more-less makes this chipset unusable? or you think this can be patched by firmware update?
jgromes 2025-10-08 1:20 a.m.
There's definitely a lot more that can be done with the FW update than for the older LoRa transceivers, that's for sure. But depends on Semtech unfortuantely
mcarper
Can we detect the bad rx in real time software side?
jgromes 2025-10-08 1:24 a.m.
It can be detected by checking if the packet starts with 4 null bytes, and if so, removing them, the rest of the packet should be valid if CRC is OK. I think checking for 0-length packets is not sufficient, at one point it happened to when previous reception was a CRC error.
1
1:25 a.m.
But I'd say it can be mitigated in a similar way to the sensitivity drop on SX126x, by restarting the Rx every once in a while
mcarper 2025-10-08 1:35 a.m.
So detection on rx is possible by doing a second pass on a failed decode. And we can clear it without a tx or is a tx needed?
mcarper
So detection on rx is possible by doing a second pass on a failed decode. And we can clear it without a tx or is a tx needed?
ViezeVingertjes 2025-10-08 1:43 a.m.
A reset should probably be sufficient _radio->standby(); _radio->startReceive();
mcarper 2025-10-08 1:45 a.m.
Until the bug is fixed upstream I think we should attempt a quick fix for this device. It's very popular and gives MeshCore a bad look unfortunately
ViezeVingertjes 2025-10-08 1:52 a.m.
For sure... in our local telegrams its a bit of a downer every time its mentioned, eventhough MT has its own major issue with LR1110, people prefer 100% not hearing certain radio's above not knowing when its deaf.
1:53 a.m.
While the only LR1110 they know/own is probably in the T1000e...
1:58 a.m.

jgromes 2025-10-08 5:44 a.m.

Mystic
are you just removing the null bytes?
ViezeVingertjes 2025-10-08 6:00 a.m.

6:00 a.m.

jgromes

Mystic 2025-10-08 7:14 a.m.
yeah i believe it’s still in the buffer. that’s one of the things i’m looking to try tonight
ViezeVingertjes
No, basically moving everything to the left for the amount of null bytes, taking in account that those always are in 4's AND a legitimate packet could start with 0x00
Mystic 2025-10-08 7:15 a.m.
but what about the bytes at the end of the packet missing from the buffer returned by radiolib?
Mystic
but what about the bytes at the end of the packet missing from the buffer returned by radiolib?
ViezeVingertjes 2025-10-08 7:58 a.m.
If it writes at an offset, i feel like it would still write the full packet, a least on radiolib depth. Would be odd if it doesn't know about the offset, yet somehow subtracts the length of it or something?
8:00 a.m.
I don't know haha, but is one to check i think... If we wouldn't be able to recover that packet, then were fully depending on semtech for a solution. (edited)
jgromes
Then it would be interesting to know what happens if we try to read additional 4 bytes then, if it's in the buffer or truly lost. Would need to skip clearing the Rx buffer in readData method.
ViezeVingertjes 2025-10-08 8:05 a.m.
Also hinting to this message ^^
ViezeVingertjes
If it writes at an offset, i feel like it would still write the full packet, a least on radiolib depth. Would be odd if it doesn't know about the offset, yet somehow subtracts the length of it or something?
Mystic 2025-10-08 9:02 a.m.
iirc radiolib gets the current offset from the radio then requests from the radio len number of bytes from its rx buffer starting at that offset and places it in the buffer you provide. thus, the end of the shifted packet is missing from the data returned by radiolib. i have some ideas, will try very soon
Mystic 2025-10-08 10:42 a.m.
okay i think i've written a workaround, now testing
2
Mystic
okay i think i've written a workaround, now testing
recrof 2025-10-08 10:54 a.m.
great news!
Mystic
but what about the bytes at the end of the packet missing from the buffer returned by radiolib?
jgromes 2025-10-08 11:44 a.m.
You should still be able to get those out by reading len+4 bytes when calling readData. The current version of the driver will unfortunately try to clear the Rx buffer though. So you would always have to read extra 4 bytes when using LR1110
11:45 a.m.
Clearing the buffer is probably why we are always seeing 4 null bytes as opposed to random garbage or the end of the previous packet
mcarper 2025-10-08 11:53 a.m.
So you might be able to detect this issue even earlier as the 4 null byte would be at the end of the last message?
jgromes
You should still be able to get those out by reading len+4 bytes when calling readData. The current version of the driver will unfortunately try to clear the Rx buffer though. So you would always have to read extra 4 bytes when using LR1110
Mystic 2025-10-08 12:23 p.m.
not directly i think - readData won’t read more bytes than the radio’s reported msg length iirc, the specified length is just a maximum
12:24 p.m.
i wrote an override for readData to patch it a little, now waiting to see if it works
12:27 p.m.
pretty much what i do is after getPacketLength, if reported length is 0, assume its a pre-shift bugged packet so increment a shiftCount counter by 1. might be able to do better with irqs or something rather than assuming all 0 length = bug (edited)
12:29 p.m.
then when reading data i first read the region in rx buffer we believe should be zeroes based on shiftCounter. if non-null bytes found, set shiftCounter to estimated value. then, do the actual read but at offset + (shiftCounter * 4) (edited)
12:31 p.m.
might be able to skip checking if expected null bytes are all truely null by resetting shiftCounter to 0 in every case that'll reset the buffer base (such as radio reset?)
12:32 p.m.
then that'll ensure it all works properly even when packets genuinely start with 4+ nulls
recrof 2025-10-08 3:15 p.m.
@Mystic anything to report?
Mystic 2025-10-08 3:15 p.m.
haven't seen the issue occur yet
3:15 p.m.
waiting
1
Mystic 2025-10-08 4:54 p.m.
chat i think it worked (edited)
4:56 p.m.
looks like i got a genuine 0-length packet too before as there was no shift afterwards so my code automatically undid the correction (edited)
4:57 p.m.
difference between true 0-length and bugged packet seems to be 0x00000038 vs 0x08000078 irq, so i can check that too before assuming its a shift. idk what that irq is, maybe someone could look into it? (edited)
4:57 p.m.
haven't yet tested in meshcore codebase where its also txing, will probably do that tmr (edited)
recrof 2025-10-08 5:03 p.m.
interesting. maybe @jgromes knows?
ViezeVingertjes 2025-10-08 5:25 p.m.
RADIOLIB_LR11X0_IRQ_LORA_RX_TIMESTAMP if i see it correctly
5:25 p.m.
src\modules\LR11x0\LR11x0.h
Mystic 2025-10-08 6:17 p.m.
2nd shift, again 0x08000078 irq, was corrected for automatically
2
Mystic
2nd shift, again 0x08000078 irq, was corrected for automatically
recrof 2025-10-08 6:21 p.m.
not all heroes wear capes!
2
Mystic 2025-10-08 6:41 p.m.
alright i'm going to bed, todos for tomorrow:
  • review logs, ensure 100% of shifts start with a 0 length packet with RADIOLIB_LR11X0_IRQ_LORA_RX_TIMESTAMP set
    • if so, check that irq before incrementing shiftCounter
  • test on repeater (repeat off) or companion firmware
  • possibly reset shiftCounter on tx or any other case that is guaranteed to reset shift
2
6:43 p.m.
current code for CustomLR1110 class: uint8_t shiftCount = 0; int16_t readData(uint8_t *data, size_t len) override { // check active modem ... (unchanged radiolib code) // get packet length // the offset is needed since LR11x0 seems to move the buffer base by 4 bytes on every packet uint8_t offset = 0; size_t length = getPacketLength(true, &offset); if (length == 0) { Serial.printf("got packet length 0, assuming shift. irq=0x%08X\n", irq); // assume this is a bug packet causing shift this->shiftCount++; if (this->shiftCount == (256/4)) this->shiftCount = 0; } else { if((len != 0) && (len < length)) { // user requested less data than we got, only return what was requested length = len; } // read the bytes expected to be null and verify that they are // if non-null byte found, update shiftCount size_t hasRead = 0; while (hasRead < this->shiftCount * 4) { size_t canRead = min((this->shiftCount * 4) - hasRead, len); state = readBuffer8(data, canRead, offset + hasRead); RADIOLIB_ASSERT(state); hasRead += canRead; for (size_t i = 0; i < canRead; i++) { if (data[i] != 0) { Serial.printf("backing off shiftCount from %d to %d due to non-null byte 0x%02X at pos %d\n", this->shiftCount, i / 4, data[i], hasRead - canRead + i); this->shiftCount = i / 4; hasRead = this->shiftCount * 4; break; } } } // read packet data state = readBuffer8(data, length, offset + (shiftCount * 4)); RADIOLIB_ASSERT(state); } // clear the Rx buffer state = clearRxBuffer(); RADIOLIB_ASSERT(state); ... (unchanged radiolib code) return(state); }

10:55 a.m.
means i cant reliably tell when a shift has truly occurred without looking at packet data
10:56 a.m.
should be fine for meshcore as shouldn't be many packets starting with 0x00000000 but not something i would integrate into radiolib

yeah that's annoying as every app needs to patch it by themselves
mcarper 2025-10-09 11:12 a.m.

8:32 a.m.
saw it hadn't worked friday morning but i've been out all day friday and saturday
8:32 a.m.

8:33 a.m.

Mystic

recrof 2025-10-12 8:58 a.m.

Mystic 2025-10-12 9:34 a.m.
i think it just worked
9:34 a.m.
'got possible bug packet' means len 0 and RADIOLIB_LR11X0_IRQ_HEADER_ERR
9:35 a.m.
so shiftCount was incremented
9:35 a.m.
next packet was valid
9:36 a.m.
and after tx shiftCount was reset and next packet was also valid
9:43 a.m.
will leave it running for a while without txing and see how it goes

Mystic 2025-10-12 11:10 a.m.
https://github.com/Woodie-07/MeshCore/commit/8426fddcb7af7243f97c665067e6ed4d03910f97
workaround for LR1110 shift issue · Woodie-07/MeshCore@8426fdd
it seems that if the LR1110 radio hears a packet corrupted in a specific way, it&amp;#39;ll report a packet of 0 length and with the header error IRQ set. every packet received afterwards will then...

Mystic

4
jgromes 2025-10-12 3:20 p.m.
FWIW I tested out switching Rx to standby to Rx on invalid header IRQ and it seems to fix the issue (fix as in the null bytes will be removed from the subsequent packets). The original packet is lost in this approach though. The switching also seems to improve resilience to the bug - with two ongoing transmissions 60 kHz apart I can trigger the issue pretty reliably (within a couple minutes), but with regular switching it ran for several hours without triggering it.
3:21 p.m.
Still no word from Semtech unfortunately
Mystic 2025-10-12 3:30 p.m.
i do something similar with my patch - on invalid header irq, assume shift has occurred; when standby called, assume shift has cleared
3:30 p.m.
nice to have confirmation that the standby call is what resets it
Billy 2025-10-12 4:39 p.m.
Awesome to see!
Billy 2025-10-12 5:42 p.m.

clauwn - BSmesh.de

Mystic 2025-10-15 4:22 p.m.
yep also been running on mine for a few days, the bug has triggered quite a few times but the patch has been working well as subsequent msgs continue coming in as normal rather than packets being shifted like before
1
4:22 p.m.
good to hear its working well for others too
Mystic
yep also been running on mine for a few days, the bug has triggered quite a few times but the patch has been working well as subsequent msgs continue coming in as normal rather than packets being shifted like before
recrof 2025-10-15 4:28 p.m.
how complex is the patch really?
4:28 p.m.
any data loss?
recrof
how complex is the patch really?
Mystic 2025-10-15 4:32 p.m.
basically it adds a u8 counter to the customlr1110 radio class which is the number of bytes shifted when RadioLibWrapper::recvRaw calls getPacketLength on the radio, it calls the override i wrote which calls it on the base class, then if its 0 and header err irq is set it increments the counter by 4 then when recvRaw calls readData on the radio, it calls the override which is basically a copy paste of radiolib's readData but with the counter added to the radio's reported offset in the call to readBuffer8 (reads bytes from the radio's rx buffer) to correct for the shift (edited)
2
recrof 2025-10-15 4:33 p.m.
great work mate
4:33 p.m.
looks as solid fix
recrof
any data loss?
Mystic 2025-10-15 4:33 p.m.
no, only thing you lose is the packet with header err irq but that was gone anyway. then, all subsequent packets are read as normal just with the offset added to correct for shift, no data loss
Mystic
basically it adds a u8 counter to the customlr1110 radio class which is the number of bytes shifted when RadioLibWrapper::recvRaw calls getPacketLength on the radio, it calls the override i wrote which calls it on the base class, then if its 0 and header err irq is set it increments the counter by 4 then when recvRaw calls readData on the radio, it calls the override which is basically a copy paste of radiolib's readData but with the counter added to the radio's reported offset in the call to readBuffer8 (reads bytes from the radio's rx buffer) to correct for the shift (edited)
Mystic 2025-10-15 4:39 p.m.
oh and it also overrides standby to reset the shift counter, which is called from within radiolib after tx
4:41 p.m.
thing is, if the shift counter is incremented or reset at the wrong time it'll corrupt the packets
4:43 p.m.
but in my testing, all seems well - shift happens on every header err irq and under no other circumstances and the shift is reset every time standby is called and under no other circumstances
Mystic
basically it adds a u8 counter to the customlr1110 radio class which is the number of bytes shifted when RadioLibWrapper::recvRaw calls getPacketLength on the radio, it calls the override i wrote which calls it on the base class, then if its 0 and header err irq is set it increments the counter by 4 then when recvRaw calls readData on the radio, it calls the override which is basically a copy paste of radiolib's readData but with the counter added to the radio's reported offset in the call to readBuffer8 (reads bytes from the radio's rx buffer) to correct for the shift (edited)

4:47 p.m.

Mystic

che aporeps 🌮 2025-10-15 4:49 p.m.

Billy
Silly question, how would I go about building it into a UF2 to flash to the T1000-e? (I already have it flashed, but a friend earlier was asking and I still have it setup locally) (edited)
Mystic 2025-10-15 4:49 p.m.
you can use the build.sh script i believe. e.g. FIRMWARE_VERSION=1.9.1 ./build.sh build-firmware t1000e_companion_radio_ble. will output the uf2 in out/
Mystic
you can use the build.sh script i believe. e.g. FIRMWARE_VERSION=1.9.1 ./build.sh build-firmware t1000e_companion_radio_ble. will output the uf2 in out/

8472 2025-10-16 3:08 a.m.
I've installed this but now have new issues that going back to the lastest standard bootloader won't resolve... Before I could just flash new firmware and it would boot straight up, now since trying the new bootloader I have to erase flash and install the firmware every time. Even if I then overwrite with exactly the same firmware it won't work without erasing the flash and doing again. It also does not seem to always boot up, and I need to again, erase flash and install the firmware. Before, while debugging and testing, I was able to just flash and it would be fine... Any ideas? The standard bootloader doesn't resolve the issue now either....
1
8472
I've installed this but now have new issues that going back to the lastest standard bootloader won't resolve... Before I could just flash new firmware and it would boot straight up, now since trying the new bootloader I have to erase flash and install the firmware every time. Even if I then overwrite with exactly the same firmware it won't work without erasing the flash and doing again. It also does not seem to always boot up, and I need to again, erase flash and install the firmware. Before, while debugging and testing, I was able to just flash and it would be fine... Any ideas? The standard bootloader doesn't resolve the issue now either....
che aporeps 🌮 2025-10-16 3:13 a.m.

che aporeps 🌮
That's the first I've heard of this problem but maybe not many people run otafix bootloader on t1000e. How busy is the mesh where you are, do you have lots of contacts?
8472 2025-10-16 3:16 a.m.

I've just flashed the standard bootloader again, erased flash and installed my build - working. Now I just reflash it again, and it won't boot up anymore.. I am going to have to erase the flash and then install it again.. What's super strange is that it doesn't work with the original bootloader anymore like it did. (edited)
8472
Quite a few, but I'm not even getting to the stage to worry about that yet... This is all over the last 45 minutes, before I even start to register any contacts. I've just flashed the standard bootloader again, erased flash and installed my build - working. Now I just reflash it again, and it won't boot up anymore.. I am going to have to erase the flash and then install it again.. What's super strange is that it doesn't work with the original bootloader anymore like it did. (edited)

recrof 2025-10-23 3:22 p.m.

Woodie 2025-10-24 1:12 p.m.
unfortunately my workaround isn't perfect, seems in some cases header_err does not result in a shift but my patch assumes it does so all subsequent packets are shifted in the opposite direction
1:13 p.m.
and in this case, repeaters will not automatically fix themselves as the packets are totally invalid so won't retransmit
1:15 p.m.
maybe better workaround would be on header err, standby(), thus returning the radio to a known-good state
1:15 p.m.
will get to work on that and submit a pr once done
Woodie 2025-10-24 1:40 p.m.
now testing new fix
1
Woodie 2025-10-24 3:22 p.m.
https://github.com/meshcore-dev/MeshCore/pull/1012
New workaround for LR1110 shift issue by Woodie-07 · Pull Request ...
My previous workaround in PR #953 worked by assuming all subsequent packets would be shifted 4 bytes to the right after a packet with the header error IRQ set so compensated by reading 4 bytes to t...
1
mrlm 2025-10-24 7:34 p.m.
Not perfect, but it hasn't happened once since.
7:35 p.m.
I assume this pulls the previous patch from dev
Woodie 2025-10-24 7:43 p.m.
yeah this is a replacement for the previous patch. in my testing, previous one works 90% of the time but the other 10% it makes things worse for repeaters and breaks just the same for companions.
mrlm 2025-10-24 7:51 p.m.
Yes it's obvious you would remove the other patch just making sense of what happens in dev is hard.

AG / Andrew 2025-10-25 12:22 p.m.
Need to make a version of this with no beep. It's driving me insane!! I can't test anymore
AG / Andrew
Need to make a version of this with no beep. It's driving me insane!! I can't test anymore
clauwn - BSmesh.de 2025-10-25 1:02 p.m.
You can press the button 3 times to disable the beep
1
recrof 2025-10-25 1:23 p.m.
or add -UPIN_BUZZER to the build flags
Woodie 2025-10-25 1:41 p.m.
we're still using the wrong irqs in isReceiving aren't we?
1:42 p.m.
#define LR1110_IRQ_HAS_PREAMBLE 0b0000000100 // 4 4 valid LoRa header received #define LR1110_IRQ_HEADER_VALID 0b0000010000 // 4 4 valid LoRa header received ... bool isReceiving() { uint16_t irq = getIrqStatus(); bool detected = ((irq & LR1110_IRQ_HEADER_VALID) || (irq & LR1110_IRQ_HAS_PREAMBLE)); return detected; }
1:42 p.m.
but: #define RADIOLIB_LR11X0_IRQ_TX_DONE (0x01UL << 2) // 31 0 interrupt: packet transmitted #define RADIOLIB_LR11X0_IRQ_RX_DONE (0x01UL << 3) // 31 0 packet received #define RADIOLIB_LR11X0_IRQ_PREAMBLE_DETECTED (0x01UL << 4) // 31 0 preamble detected #define RADIOLIB_LR11X0_IRQ_SYNC_WORD_HEADER_VALID (0x01UL << 5) // 31 0 sync word or LoRa header valid (edited)
1:44 p.m.
effectively, we're checking PREAMBLE_DETECTED as we should be (just named wrong) and we're checking TX_DONE instead of HEADER_VALID
1:45 p.m.
i do see a lot of CAD busy max duration reached! messages so that's probably why
1:46 p.m.
i probably should've posted in #development instead of this thread as its unrelated to the shift issue but oh well
Woodie 2025-10-25 1:56 p.m.
any reason for the defines instead of just using the radiolib defines (like RADIOLIB_LR11X0_IRQ_PREAMBLE_DETECTED) directly? (edited)
Woodie
any reason for the defines instead of just using the radiolib defines (like RADIOLIB_LR11X0_IRQ_PREAMBLE_DETECTED) directly? (edited)
recrof 2025-10-25 2:01 p.m.
not sure.. @Scott probably knows
clauwn - BSmesh.de
You can press the button 3 times to disable the beep
AG / Andrew 2025-10-25 2:06 p.m.
Wow. Thank you!
1
AG / Andrew
Wow. Thank you!
recrof 2025-10-25 2:09 p.m.
just a warning: it will shut up until next powercycle
1
1
Woodie
any reason for the defines instead of just using the radiolib defines (like RADIOLIB_LR11X0_IRQ_PREAMBLE_DETECTED) directly? (edited)
FDLamotte 2025-10-25 2:15 p.m.
I remember I checked it was the same as the one defined in radiolib but used the same way as in the existing wrappers ... should probably use the defined one directly
Woodie 2025-10-25 2:16 p.m.
it's not the same as in radiolib
2:17 p.m.
like LR1110_IRQ_HAS_PREAMBLE is defined as 0b0000000100 so 1 << 2 whereas in radiolib that's 1 << 4
FDLamotte 2025-10-25 2:24 p.m.
I remember having had headaches with that
2:24 p.m.
isn't the irq status reconstructed by radiolib ?
Woodie 2025-10-25 2:25 p.m.
no, getIrqStatus returns exactly what the radio returns
2:25 p.m.
uint32_t LR11x0::getIrqStatus() { // there is no dedicated "get IRQ" command, the IRQ bits are sent after the status bytes uint8_t buff[6] = { 0 }; this->mod->spiConfig.widths[RADIOLIB_MODULE_SPI_WIDTH_STATUS] = Module::BITS_0; mod->SPItransferStream(NULL, 0, false, NULL, buff, sizeof(buff), true); this->mod->spiConfig.widths[RADIOLIB_MODULE_SPI_WIDTH_STATUS] = Module::BITS_8; uint32_t irq = ((uint32_t)(buff[2]) << 24) | ((uint32_t)(buff[3]) << 16) | ((uint32_t)(buff[4]) << 8) | (uint32_t)buff[5]; return(irq); }
FDLamotte 2025-10-25 2:25 p.m.
ok, so I must be confusing with another piece of code
FDLamotte 2025-10-25 2:35 p.m.
yes, it should be as you say ... and it's probably my fault if it's like that
Woodie 2025-10-25 2:36 p.m.
alright i'll put in a pr
Woodie
alright i'll put in a pr

FDLamotte
while you're at it, hasn't getTimeOnAir been fixed upstream ?
Woodie 2025-10-25 3:13 p.m.
was it? i don't know what the problem was with the radiolib function but i can see the calculation has been changed a little from what radiolib had. it looks like the radiolib function was not changed until https://github.com/jgromes/RadioLib/pull/1596 which completely rewrites that function and has different results compared to the old function which it sounds like is intended and is now correct? so i assume this new rewritten function is the fix?
Add calculateTimeOnAir() method to relevant modules by GUVWAF · ...
As discussed in #1584, this adds the calculateTimeOnAir() method to SX126x, SX127x, SX128x and LR11x0 modules in order to calculate the airtime of a packet with different configurations than the cu...
Woodie
was it? i don't know what the problem was with the radiolib function but i can see the calculation has been changed a little from what radiolib had. it looks like the radiolib function was not changed until https://github.com/jgromes/RadioLib/pull/1596 which completely rewrites that function and has different results compared to the old function which it sounds like is intended and is now correct? so i assume this new rewritten function is the fix?
FDLamotte 2025-10-25 3:19 p.m.
must be that ... the values were too short and worse I think it returned 0 for preamble length of 16

Woodie 2025-10-25 5:20 p.m.
https://github.com/meshcore-dev/MeshCore/pull/1018
LR1110 IRQ fixes by Woodie-07 · Pull Request #1018 · meshcore-dev...
The isReceiving function (used for delaying TX until clear) was using incorrect IRQs meaning instead of checking the HEADER_VALID IRQ, TX_DONE was used instead. This PR removes the incorrect define...

Resilient, offline communication for field operations — built on MeshCore and LoRa mesh networking.
recrof 2025-11-14 8:21 a.m.
Forwarded
May well just be my t1000e's bad tx, but I'm on the UK mesh and I have to retry messages 5-10 times to get anything through to my local repeater. Seen other murmurings of others facing similar issues. Is this just me, or are we all doing the same?
Originally sent: 2025-11-14 8:13 a.m.
Random Citizen 2025-11-14 9:25 a.m.
just erased my T1000, flashed official and I hear my repeater repeating 99% of my messages (that 1% is when I type+enter too fast 2 messages in a row)
9:26 a.m.
my repeater is on ~1.10.0 tho
recrof 2025-11-14 9:38 a.m.
also see the range very similar to my other clients
che aporeps 🌮 2025-11-14 7:22 p.m.
I looked at let's mesh and the 24 hr average Rx ch util for UK is 10%, that's not including tx
7:23 p.m.
And obviously it's higher during the day, more like pushing 15-20% total during peak times
7:24 p.m.
So it's maybe not surprising to have trouble with messages not getting heard due to colllisions
che aporeps 🌮
And obviously it's higher during the day, more like pushing 15-20% total during peak times
mcarper 2025-11-14 7:43 p.m.
UK should try 20.8khz and SF5. Also make sure home assistant is not pulling data continuously from repeaters

ka.RFz 2025-11-19 8:50 p.m.
I had been running 1.8.0 on my t1000e's since I got them. Never once experienced that sudden deafness issue that others had reported. Upgraded to 1.10.0 last night. It was working as expected until around 1219 today, no adverts all afternoon. reset the radio through the app and boom adverts came back. All I see in the RX logs prior to the reset are TRANSPORT_FLOOD REQUEST's. Same issue?
ka.RFz 2025-11-19 8:58 p.m.
recrof 2025-11-20 1:23 a.m.
interesting. @Woodie any thoughts?
FDLamotte 2025-11-20 2:28 a.m.
If TRANSPORT_FLOOD packets are received and transport code are not used in your region there is definitely a problem
2:28 a.m.
the header for TRANSPORT_FLOOD is the same as FLOOD but with lowest bit to 0
2:32 a.m.
A TRANSPORT_FLOOD packet for a request will be a 0, then if transport codes are both set to 0 and path is null, this is a valid packet starting with 4 bytes to 0 ...
Woodie 2025-11-20 2:49 a.m.
hmm, that’s really weird
2:51 a.m.
the patch is just on header error irq -> standby radio
2:51 a.m.
i don’t see how that could cause shifts that didn’t happen before
Woodie 2025-11-20 10:56 a.m.
only possible explanation i can think of is that:
  • in 1.8.0 something was different that means the shift issue never happens (or they got very lucky), AND
  • my current patch is not perfect, the shift can still occur, because either:
    • not all shifts begin with a header error, OR
    • not all shifts can be fixed with a standby()
 (edited)
10:57 a.m.
unfortunately it sounds very difficult to reproduce
mrlm 2025-11-20 1:26 p.m.
Since updating to 1.10.0 the T1000 and the Rak Tag have both been weird. Bad BT connections, locking up and erasing their config. I had almost no issues before when running dev. Obviously only the T1000 has this radio. (edited)
mrlm
Since updating to 1.10.0 the T1000 and the Rak Tag have both been weird. Bad BT connections, locking up and erasing their config. I had almost no issues before when running dev. Obviously only the T1000 has this radio. (edited)

mrlm 2025-11-20 1:28 p.m.

ViezeVingertjes 2025-11-20 1:30 p.m.

mrlm 2025-11-20 1:31 p.m.
